**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by The Church of Scotland Faith Impact Forum (OSB81)**

**UK Parliament Online Safety Bill**

**Submitted by Dr Murdo Macdonald, Policy Adviser (Society, Religion and Technology)**

**Introduction**

The Church of Scotland welcomes the opportunity to respond to the Online Safety Bill. We support the stated aim of the Bill to "make the UK the safest place in the world to be online while defending free expression". While there is much to welcome in the Bill, we have some suggestions for improvement.

One of the challenges of the Bill is to balance the first two of the five stated policy objectives of the Bill, namely to increase user safety online while also seeking "to preserve….. freedom of speech online." We share with others previously expressed concerns about the length and complexity of the Bill, and hope that clearer definitions of some of the terms used could help protect aspects of freedom of speech \[1\].

Our response primarily addresses Part 3 (relating to social media sites/websites which contain user-to-user generated content) and Part 5 (pornographic websites) of the Bill.

**Regulation of pornographic content**
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by The Church of Scotland Faith Impact Forum (OSB81)**

**UK Parliament Online Safety Bill**

**Submitted by Dr Murdo Macdonald, Policy Adviser (Society, Religion and Technology)**

**Introduction**

The Church of Scotland welcomes the opportunity to respond to the Online Safety Bill. We support the stated aim of the Bill to "make the UK the safest place in the world to be online while defending free expression". While there is much to welcome in the Bill, we have some suggestions for improvement.

One of the challenges of the Bill is to balance the first two of the five stated policy objectives of the Bill, namely to increase user safety online while also seeking "to preserve….. freedom of speech online." We share with others previously expressed concerns about the length and complexity of the Bill, and hope that clearer definitions of some of the terms used could help protect aspects of freedom of speech \[1\].

Our response primarily addresses Part 3 (relating to social media sites/websites which contain user-to-user generated content) and Part 5 (pornographic websites) of the Bill.

**Regulation of pornographic content**

chunks.text: the Bill, and hope that clearer definitions of some of the terms used could help protect aspects of freedom of speech \[1\]. Our response primarily addresses Part 3 (relating to social media sites/websites which contain user-to-user generated content) and Part 5 (pornographic websites) of the Bill. **Regulation of pornographic content** The Part 5 regulation of pornographic websites may be open to abuse. There are currently estimated to be 4-5 million dedicated pornographic websites which can be accessed from the UK \[2\]. The intention of the Bill is that those sites which have pornographic material as their main content will be covered by the regulations in Part 5. However, others sites, such as social media sites/ sites which contain user-to-user generated content, may also contain pornographic material which, in the legislation as currently proposed, will only be covered by the more lax regulation in Part 3. We call for clarity about the scope of requirements to protect children and the duties on removing images of child sexual abuse and sexually violent material, and believe that there needs to be equivalent treatment for all online platforms.
chunks.display_text: The Part 5 regulation of pornographic websites may be open to abuse. There are currently estimated to be 4-5 million dedicated pornographic websites which can be accessed from the UK \[2\]. The intention of the Bill is that those sites which have pornographic material as their main content will be covered by the regulations in Part 5. However, others sites, such as social media sites/ sites which contain user-to-user generated content, may also contain pornographic material which, in the legislation as currently proposed, will only be covered by the more lax regulation in Part 3. We call for clarity about the scope of requirements to protect children and the duties on removing images of child sexual abuse and sexually violent material, and believe that there needs to be equivalent treatment for all online platforms.

chunks.text: only be covered by the more lax regulation in Part 3. We call for clarity about the scope of requirements to protect children and the duties on removing images of child sexual abuse and sexually violent material, and believe that there needs to be equivalent treatment for all online platforms. The harm caused to individuals and to society by pornography is indisputable. This is especially true for children: as a result, some children’s charities (such as Barnardo’s and Child net) have welcomed the Bill’s provisions on pornography. However, we note that the End Violence Against Women Coalition has criticised the current draft of the Bill for not naming violence against women and girls as a priority harm in the Bill: we would support doing so. We would also recommend that pornography as a priority harm to children should be named specifically in the Bill. While the ways in which pornography is distributed will inevitably evolve, the fact remains that digital and online access is likely to expand. The ease of access to pornography via the internet is a key harm to people under the age of 18. Thus, placing pornography as a known key harms on the face of the Bill is essential in our view. **Enforcement** A key issue around enforcement and workability of these regulations rests on age verification systems. Both Parts 3 and 5 include a duty to prevent children accessing content, and systems must be put in place by websites and providers to do so. This is to be welcomed - so long as the regulator has the resources and powers to ensure compliance, using enforcement as and when necessary.
chunks.display_text: The harm caused to individuals and to society by pornography is indisputable. This is especially true for children: as a result, some children’s charities (such as Barnardo’s and Child net) have welcomed the Bill’s provisions on pornography. However, we note that the End Violence Against Women Coalition has criticised the current draft of the Bill for not naming violence against women and girls as a priority harm in the Bill: we would support doing so. We would also recommend that pornography as a priority harm to children should be named specifically in the Bill. While the ways in which pornography is distributed will inevitably evolve, the fact remains that digital and online access is likely to expand. The ease of access to pornography via the internet is a key harm to people under the age of 18. Thus, placing pornography as a known key harms on the face of the Bill is essential in our view. **Enforcement** A key issue around enforcement and workability of these regulations rests on age verification systems. Both Parts 3 and 5 include a duty to prevent children accessing content, and systems must be put in place by websites and providers to do so. This is to be welcomed - so long as the regulator has the resources and powers to ensure compliance, using enforcement as and when necessary.

chunks.text: 3 and 5 include a duty to prevent children accessing content, and systems must be put in place by websites and providers to do so. This is to be welcomed - so long as the regulator has the resources and powers to ensure compliance, using enforcement as and when necessary. We are concerned about the lack of clarity around what this duty for age verification might entail. A tick box, assuring that the visitor to the site is of appropriate age, could meet the minimum standards of the Bill for some content, but this clearly would not be an effective or robust age verification system, and would be easily evaded. Greater clarity is required within the Bill as to what will constitute minimum standards for age verification. The Impact Assessment contains statements only of what "may" be required- well short of the robust age verification checks promised by previous Government statements that "companies \[will be expected\] to use age verification technologies to prevent children from accessing services which pose the highest risk of harm to children, such as online pornography". The Government needs to be much clearer about what the expectations (and indeed the likely costs) will be in relation to age verification, and the standards required to protect children to comply with the Bill as it progresses.
chunks.display_text: We are concerned about the lack of clarity around what this duty for age verification might entail. A tick box, assuring that the visitor to the site is of appropriate age, could meet the minimum standards of the Bill for some content, but this clearly would not be an effective or robust age verification system, and would be easily evaded. Greater clarity is required within the Bill as to what will constitute minimum standards for age verification. The Impact Assessment contains statements only of what "may" be required- well short of the robust age verification checks promised by previous Government statements that "companies \[will be expected\] to use age verification technologies to prevent children from accessing services which pose the highest risk of harm to children, such as online pornography". The Government needs to be much clearer about what the expectations (and indeed the likely costs) will be in relation to age verification, and the standards required to protect children to comply with the Bill as it progresses.

chunks.text: pose the highest risk of harm to children, such as online pornography". The Government needs to be much clearer about what the expectations (and indeed the likely costs) will be in relation to age verification, and the standards required to protect children to comply with the Bill as it progresses. In addition, we are concerned that there appear to be contradictions in how guidance for age verification requirements will be determined between Part 3 and Part 5. If different requirements are imposed between the two Parts of the Bill, this has the potential to make the system unworkable, since some websites could be regulated under both parts 3 and 5. Of particular concern is that, while regulation under Part 3 has a measure of Parliamentary scrutiny, for Part 5 (relating to pornographic websites), OFCOM will determine, without any Parliamentary scrutiny, what will be acceptable age verification. It is proposed that this will be done following consultation- including with groups representing adult users, some of whom may have a commercial or business interest in lobbying for more lax regulation. We recommend that the Codes of Practice and Guidance on age verification for both Parts 3 and 5 be compatible, and that both should have some level of Parliamentary oversight. **Timetables**
chunks.display_text: In addition, we are concerned that there appear to be contradictions in how guidance for age verification requirements will be determined between Part 3 and Part 5. If different requirements are imposed between the two Parts of the Bill, this has the potential to make the system unworkable, since some websites could be regulated under both parts 3 and 5. Of particular concern is that, while regulation under Part 3 has a measure of Parliamentary scrutiny, for Part 5 (relating to pornographic websites), OFCOM will determine, without any Parliamentary scrutiny, what will be acceptable age verification. It is proposed that this will be done following consultation- including with groups representing adult users, some of whom may have a commercial or business interest in lobbying for more lax regulation. We recommend that the Codes of Practice and Guidance on age verification for both Parts 3 and 5 be compatible, and that both should have some level of Parliamentary oversight. **Timetables**

chunks.text: representing adult users, some of whom may have a commercial or business interest in lobbying for more lax regulation. We recommend that the Codes of Practice and Guidance on age verification for both Parts 3 and 5 be compatible, and that both should have some level of Parliamentary oversight. **Timetables** There has already been significant delay in developing this legislation, meaning that vulnerable children remain unprotected. In the three years since the White Paper, the Government has had ample time to form its outline views on which companies will be subject to which regimes. In addition, the continuing uncertainty around standards, systems and costs makes it difficult for businesses to prepare to implement the requirements as soon as possible. The Government should give broad indications of these proposed systems and categories, reducing uncertainty and making more Parliamentary time to debate practical issues. We recommend that effective and ambitions timetables for commencement be developed and implemented. As Part 5 requires no secondary legislation, and given the level of public support for effective age verification, OFCOM should be working on the guidance for the implementation of Part 5 while the Bill progresses through Parliament. This guidance could be put out for consultation immediately following Royal Assent, and then laid before Parliament. This would allow for Part 5 legislation to become effective soon after passage of the Bill. Implementation of Part 3 will require Regulations and Codes of Practice. To prevent further slippage (on current timetables, implementation could be 2025 or later), we would suggest that it would be preferable if a commencement clause for Parts 3 and 5 within a specified period (e.g. six months of Royal Assent for Part 5, one year for Part 3) were on the face of the Bill. **Oversight**
chunks.display_text: There has already been significant delay in developing this legislation, meaning that vulnerable children remain unprotected. In the three years since the White Paper, the Government has had ample time to form its outline views on which companies will be subject to which regimes. In addition, the continuing uncertainty around standards, systems and costs makes it difficult for businesses to prepare to implement the requirements as soon as possible. The Government should give broad indications of these proposed systems and categories, reducing uncertainty and making more Parliamentary time to debate practical issues. We recommend that effective and ambitions timetables for commencement be developed and implemented. As Part 5 requires no secondary legislation, and given the level of public support for effective age verification, OFCOM should be working on the guidance for the implementation of Part 5 while the Bill progresses through Parliament. This guidance could be put out for consultation immediately following Royal Assent, and then laid before Parliament. This would allow for Part 5 legislation to become effective soon after passage of the Bill. Implementation of Part 3 will require Regulations and Codes of Practice. To prevent further slippage (on current timetables, implementation could be 2025 or later), we would suggest that it would be preferable if a commencement clause for Parts 3 and 5 within a specified period (e.g. six months of Royal Assent for Part 5, one year for Part 3) were on the face of the Bill. **Oversight**

chunks.text: timetables, implementation could be 2025 or later), we would suggest that it would be preferable if a commencement clause for Parts 3 and 5 within a specified period (e.g. six months of Royal Assent for Part 5, one year for Part 3) were on the face of the Bill. **Oversight** We have seen the use by the state in other jurisdictions of their ability to limit online access to information which it deems undesirable (such as in China), and also in states such as Myanmar to switch off the internet service altogether. While it may seem unlikely that this kind of heavy-handed tactic might be employed by the UK state (at least in the short term), we are conscious that the legislation as currently framed means that this may be possible. We are therefore concerned about the lack of clarity over the power that the Secretary of State has over redacting or otherwise influencing the availability of information. We are aware that, for most people, online information is the most readily available (and often the only) source of information. While the state has a role in protecting citizens by prohibiting the circulation of false or harmful information, the possibility for government- directed disinformation or censorship in order to "nudge" or otherwise influence is a real and dangerous one. Where the Secretary of State does deem it in the public interest, we would see a role for Parliament (working with the devolved Governments and Parliaments) or the Courts to provide a degree of oversight or authorisation. **Other issues** The Bill seeks to impose duties on "regulated services" (user-to-user services which share user generated content (such as Facebook) and search services (e.g. Google) which are accessible within the UK) in relation to three types of content: • illegal content.
chunks.display_text: We have seen the use by the state in other jurisdictions of their ability to limit online access to information which it deems undesirable (such as in China), and also in states such as Myanmar to switch off the internet service altogether. While it may seem unlikely that this kind of heavy-handed tactic might be employed by the UK state (at least in the short term), we are conscious that the legislation as currently framed means that this may be possible. We are therefore concerned about the lack of clarity over the power that the Secretary of State has over redacting or otherwise influencing the availability of information. We are aware that, for most people, online information is the most readily available (and often the only) source of information. While the state has a role in protecting citizens by prohibiting the circulation of false or harmful information, the possibility for government- directed disinformation or censorship in order to "nudge" or otherwise influence is a real and dangerous one. Where the Secretary of State does deem it in the public interest, we would see a role for Parliament (working with the devolved Governments and Parliaments) or the Courts to provide a degree of oversight or authorisation. **Other issues** The Bill seeks to impose duties on "regulated services" (user-to-user services which share user generated content (such as Facebook) and search services (e.g. Google) which are accessible within the UK) in relation to three types of content: • illegal content.

chunks.text: to provide a degree of oversight or authorisation. **Other issues** The Bill seeks to impose duties on "regulated services" (user-to-user services which share user generated content (such as Facebook) and search services (e.g. Google) which are accessible within the UK) in relation to three types of content: • illegal content. • content that is harmful to children. • content that is legal but harmful to adults. While the Church obviously welcomes curbs on content which is illegal or harmful to children, there has been some disquiet expressed by many about the Government’s current proposals around content that may be "legal but harmful" to adults, as this potentially has impacts on freedom of speech. Of particular concern is the vague indication that content considered "likely" to result in "psychological harm" would be removed or banned, with no requirement to prove any actual or potential harm. Striking the balance between the rights of people, organisations or companies to express their views, and of users who are (or feel) excluded or silenced by non-criminal speech, is undoubtedly challenging. Social and cultural mores change, so that what was deemed acceptable even a few decades ago might now, while still being within the law, be considered by some to be harmful and thus proscribed- or may become so in the future, and thus fall foul of this legislation. *17th June 2022* --- \[1\] https://www.cyberleagle.com/2022/03/mapping-online-safety-bill.html \[2\] Online Safety Bill – Impact Assessment (IA) (parliament.uk) p41
chunks.display_text: • content that is harmful to children. • content that is legal but harmful to adults. Of particular concern is the vague indication that content considered "likely" to result in "psychological harm" would be removed or banned, with no requirement to prove any actual or potential harm. Striking the balance between the rights of people, organisations or companies to express their views, and of users who are (or feel) excluded or silenced by non-criminal speech, is undoubtedly challenging.
